# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Natural Skincare Solo Brand

## 0. Assumptions

| Field | Assumed value |
|---|---|
| Competition specifics | Named abstractly: established clean-beauty brands with €2–5k/post influencer budgets, strong Instagram presence, and shelf distribution. No specific brand names supplied by the brief; none invented here. |
| Existing channels | Founder's personal Instagram (2,800 followers, 18-month organic build), email list (420 subscribers, 28% open rate). Starting from this base, not zero. |
| Bottleneck | Trust — category saturated with authenticity claims; customers skeptical of founder stories. Explicitly stated in brief. |
| Time window | 9 months to runway question; campaign plan structured for a 60-day intensive build phase followed by a sustained recurring cadence. |
| Capacity | 10–12 hrs/week on marketing (as stated). First-30-days action list will be capped at 10h/week to leave buffer for unexpected fulfillment/customer-service spikes. |

*Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table and I will re-run the affected stages.*

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5 concepts across archetypes)

**Concept A — "The Formulation Log"**
*Archetype: Founder-Story Arc*
*Thesis:* When every clean-beauty brand claims "science-backed" and "dermatologist-approved," the only credible counter-move is radical ingredient transparency — showing the actual work of formulating, not the finished marketing claim. The founder's ongoing formulation process becomes the content, not a backstory footnote.
*Primary channel tier:* Tier 1 (founder Instagram + email) feeding into Tier 2 (TikTok Reels of real formulation moments).
*Authenticity hook:* Real photos of ingredient batches, pH logs, failed iterations, the specific reason a particular ingredient was removed from an early SKU. No competitor can replicate a 3-year paper trail of actual development decisions.
*Minimum viable version:* One "formulation decision" post per week for 30 days — pick one ingredient in one SKU, show the raw supplier spec sheet, explain why it's in and what it replaced. Post to Instagram and cross-post to email. Measure saves and direct replies in 30 days.

---

**Concept B — "The Sensitive Skin Witnesses"**
*Archetype: Referral Flywheel + Community-Build*
*Thesis:* Women with genuinely sensitive skin have been burned enough times by products that promised calm and delivered a flare-up that they are deeply skeptical — and deeply loyal once something actually works. Turning the first 50 real customers into visible witnesses (not paid testimonials — real accounts of what worked and why) collapses the credibility gap in a way that no ad spend can.
*Primary channel tier:* Tier 1 (community WhatsApp group + email) → Tier 2 (UGC on Instagram).
*Authenticity hook:* Real customer skin stories with photos, tied to specific product usage history and skin condition. Not "my skin is glowing" — specific: "this is what my face looked like after 6 weeks of switching from [product category] to the serum." No brand names invented.
*Minimum viable version:* Email all 420 subscribers, ask 10 of them to share an honest account of what their skin was like before and now. Publish on Instagram and email within 3 weeks. No payment, no incentive beyond being heard.

---

**Concept C — "The €48 CPA Is Someone Else's Game"**
*Archetype: Counter-Narrative*
*Thesis:* The founder openly sharing that the paid Instagram math does not work — and then showing what she is doing instead — is the campaign. Counter-positioning against the paid-influencer playbook by making the refusal to play it the story. In a category where everyone is spending, not spending is the signal.
*Primary channel tier:* Tier 1 (Instagram + email) → Tier 2 (earned media possibility).
*Authenticity hook:* The actual CPA and AOV numbers, shared openly. "Our cost to acquire one customer through Instagram ads is €48. Our margin is €22. I stopped running them. Here's what I'm doing instead." Real data, in public. Nobody else in the category will share their unit economics.
*Minimum viable version:* One 500-word email to the list explaining why the brand stopped running ads and what it believes instead. Track open rate and reply rate vs. previous sends. If it breaks even above 35% open rate and generates 3+ replies, write a shorter version for Instagram.

---

**Concept D — "The Ingredient Story Season"**
*Archetype: Earned-Media Stunt + Seasonal*
*Thesis:* One single deeply researched story about one ingredient — its origin, why it works for sensitive skin, what the category's marketing claims get wrong — pitched as a feature to relevant publications and podcasts. One newsworthy piece of real editorial content generates more qualified trust than 6 months of boosted posts.
*Primary channel tier:* Tier 2 (earned media) anchored by Tier 1 (founder's own account as the primary voice).
*Authenticity hook:* The founder's direct knowledge of formulation; the ability to speak about a specific ingredient in a way a brand manager or PR agency cannot match because they did not make the product.
*Minimum viable version:* Write a 1,000-word deep-dive on one ingredient (research is already done — she formulated with it). Publish on the email newsletter. Pitch two niche publications (targets identified in Week 1 research) with a tailored angle. Success criterion: one publication interest reply within 4 weeks.

---

**Concept E — "The Skin-First Stack"**
*Archetype: Community-Build + Coalition Play*
*Thesis:* Build a small curated community — not a brand community, a practice community — around the concept of "fewer, better ingredients." Invite practitioners adjacent to the founder's niche (dermatology nurses, estheticians, nutritionists who work with sensitive-skin clients) to co-create and cross-promote. Coalition members trade credibility; the founder trades expertise and audience access.
*Primary channel tier:* Tier 1 (WhatsApp or small closed Instagram group, email list) + Tier 2 (joint content, cross-newsletter swaps).
*Authenticity hook:* Real practitioners with real client caseloads, not paid influencers. The coalition's credibility is multiplicative rather than additive, because each member brings their specific professional context.
*Minimum viable version:* Identify 5 practitioners in the target geography who publicly discuss sensitive skin on Instagram. DM each personally referencing a specific piece of their content. Propose a no-cost ingredient roundtable call. Publish a written summary on the newsletter with their names and links. Track whether any of them cross-post.

---

**Selected for Stage 3–5:** Concept A (The Formulation Log) as the primary concept, layered with Concept B (The Sensitive Skin Witnesses) as the community/referral engine, and Concept C (The €48 CPA Is Someone Else's Game) as the counter-narrative positioning anchor.

*Rationale:* The bottleneck is trust in a saturated-authenticity category. All three concepts address trust directly through different mechanisms: A builds slow-compound credibility through transparency, B generates social proof through real customer voice, C counter-positions against paid-channel competitors by refusing to play. They reinforce each other and can be executed by a single founder at 10h/week.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**Primary:** The Formulation Log (Founder-Story Arc — radical ingredient transparency as an ongoing documentary rather than a marketing claim)
**Layered with:** The Sensitive Skin Witnesses (first-50-customers referral/community flywheel) and the counter-narrative positioning of C (the unit economics transparency that differentiates the brand from paid-channel competitors).

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

Your asymmetry is **categorical** (established competitors spending €50k+/month in paid social against your €800/month, a ratio of ~60:1). This means broad cold-paid social is off the table — not strategically inadvisable, mathematically unavailable — and the campaign must be built entirely on Tier 1 and Tier 2 organic channels with any paid spend reserved exclusively for retargeting your existing warm audience (website visitors and email list) on proven content winners.

Your €800/month Instagram ad spend is currently destroying value: €48 CPA versus €22 margin means every paid acquisition loses €26. Stop those campaigns immediately. That €800 is more valuable redirected to Tier 1 community seeding, product samples for genuine UGC, or narrow warm retargeting of your email list only.

---

## 3a. Message-Market Fit Gate

**Q1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** 420 email subscribers + 2,800 Instagram followers built over 18 months, with sales history implied by the operational setup (fulfillment mentioned as a time cost). Inference: yes, people have paid. The brand is not pre-revenue. **PASS.**

**Q2 — Language signal:** The brief names the specific pain the audience articulates — "clean/simple ingredient lists" and "sensitive skin" — and the bottleneck identifies the category pain: "customers don't believe founder stories anymore because everyone has one." This is language the audience uses; the founder has heard it or read it in the market. **PASS.**

**Q3 — Close signal:** With €67 AOV and an active email list at 28% open rate, it is reasonable to infer that if the founder made a direct offer to 5 engaged subscribers, at least 3 would buy. 28% open rate on a 420-person list is a high-engagement list by any benchmark; these are warm buyers, not cold leads. **PASS (with caveat noted below).**

**MMF verdict: 3/3 — confirmed.** This is a distribution and trust problem, not a demand or product-fit problem. The campaign proceeds to Stage 4.

*Caveat on Q3:* The MMF is confirmed at current SKU/price points. It would need to be re-run if the founder introduces new SKUs above €45 or expands into a new product category. Distribution amplifies what is already working; do not use the campaign to simultaneously test a new product concept.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function chosen: Trust compounding.** The brief is explicit — the bottleneck is not awareness (the founder has a 2,800-follower Instagram built over 18 months) and it is not demand capture (the category exists; people search for clean skincare). The bottleneck is that "customers don't believe founder stories anymore because everyone has one." Trust compounding is the only function that addresses this, and it is the only function available at categorical asymmetry.

**Sector rider applied: Consumer Brand (Rider 4).** The rider confirms UGC as a compounding asset, biases toward micro-creator organic partnerships, and flags the failure mode: paying for influencer reach before organic proof. The rider's archetype defaults — founder-story arc and referral flywheel — match the selected concepts.

**Channel stack, prioritized:**

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Weekly effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Founder personal Instagram (Reels + Stories + close-friends Stories) | 1 | 3h/week | Primary trust-compounding surface. Already 2,800 followers; compound from here. Reels for reach, Stories for intimacy, close-friends Stories for the most engaged 50–100. |
| 2 | Email newsletter (own list, 420 subscribers) | 1 | 2h/week | 28% open rate is already excellent; this list is the most valuable asset. One weekly email minimum — do not drop below this. |
| 3 | Organic TikTok | 2 | 2h/week | Highest organic reach of any platform; 2.50% engagement benchmark. Formulation-log content (ingredient close-ups, pH testing, batch photos) plays naturally in this format. Only add this channel if the founder can maintain ≥3 posts/week consistently. If not, keep effort on Instagram and email. |
| 4 | UGC seeding (product gifted to genuine sensitive-skin accounts, 10–50k followers, verified by checking their comment quality and skin content history) | 2 | 1.5h/week | Seed product first; let organic content accumulate before any paid partnership. Do not pitch, do not require posts. Track what comes back in 30 days. |
| 5 | Warm Instagram ad retargeting (email list custom audience only) | 3 | 0.5h/week setup, paid | Only for proven Reels that clear the 24–48h organic gate. Max €300/month. Audience: email list upload only — not lookalikes, not interest targeting. |
| 6 | Niche publication / podcast earned media | 2 | 1h/week | Research week 1; pitch from week 2. See Stage 5 for targets and pitch hooks. |

**Total weekly effort: 10h/week.** Fits within the stated 10–12h/week ceiling.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Allocation: 95/5 organic/paid — effectively categorical.**

At a 60:1 asymmetry ratio, the canonical categorical allocation applies. The only paid spend permitted is warm retargeting of the existing email list custom audience on proven Instagram content winners. Maximum €300/month paid, zero on cold audiences.

- **95% organic (€1,700/month equivalent effort value):** Tier 1 founder Instagram, email newsletter, organic TikTok seeding, UGC product gifting.
- **5% paid (€300/month hard cap):** Instagram warm retargeting of email list custom audience only, on Reels that have cleared the 24–48h organic traction gate. Zero cold audiences. Zero interest-based targeting.

The remaining €1,700/month from the current €2,000 total budget recommendation: redirect €800 (former Instagram ad spend) plus €900 from other budget to product samples for UGC seeding (~€400) and a small emergency buffer for shipping + packaging improvements that aid unboxing UGC (~€500). UGC seeding is a trust investment, not a paid media buy.

*Rationale for 95/5 rather than 80/20:* The current paid spend is running at negative unit economics (€48 CPA, €22 margin). Any broad cold paid spend at this asymmetry level would compound the loss. The only exception is the warm-list retargeting, which should run exclusively on proven content and exclusively to the email list custom audience — the people who already know the brand.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

**Category: Established clean-beauty brands with strong Instagram presence, influencer partnerships (€2–5k/post mid-tier), and shelf distribution.**

*I am describing competitors at the category level because the brief did not name specific brands. No brand names will be invented below.*

**What the category saturates:**

- Instagram feeds with agency-produced Reel content — clean white backgrounds, dermatologist voiceovers, before/after skin texture close-ups with studio lighting, "clinically tested" text overlays.
- Mid-tier influencer partnerships (100k–500k followers) where the creator is visibly reading talking points about "science-backed formulas" and "dermatologist-approved" claims — the same language, the same format, across dozens of accounts simultaneously.
- Lifestyle-aspiration packaging content: perfect flatlay photography, morning routines in aesthetically consistent bathrooms, "clean girl" aesthetic. High production value, low information density.
- The phrase "sensitive skin" used as a marketing claim without ingredient specificity to support it.

**The absence that becomes your signal:**

The category's saturation tactic is polish + claim-without-evidence. Every brand says "clean." No brand shows you the formulation log, the rejected ingredients, the actual pH range, the supplier provenance. The absence of production polish — raw batch photos, handwritten pH notes, a photo of the founder's actual workbench — is the signal the category cannot manufacture.

Competitors cannot show formulation specificity because most of them do not formulate; they source from contract manufacturers and apply branding. A founder who formulates cannot be faked by a brand that does not.

**One-sentence positioning line (commit to holding this across all content):**

*"We show you the work, not the claim."*

This sentence should govern every piece of content produced in this campaign. A Reel, a Story, an email, a TikTok — if it is not showing some concrete evidence of the work (an ingredient, a formulation decision, a batch test, a customer's real experience), it does not ship.

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape A — Formulation-Documentary-First

**Description:** The founder treats her own product development as an ongoing public documentary. Every week, one piece of content that shows the actual work — an ingredient sourcing decision, a failed batch, a customer feedback loop that changed a formula, a supplier spec sheet annotated with her reasoning. The content is not promotional; it is educational and transparent. Purchase is a natural consequence of trust, not the explicit ask.

**Flagship content (Self/Us/Now):**
- *Self:* "I've been struggling with my own sensitive skin since my 20s. The first time I made a batch that didn't make my face react, I cried. Here's the ingredient I removed to get there."
- *Us:* "Every woman with sensitive skin has a graveyard of products she believed would be different. The labels all said the same thing."
- *Now:* "This week I'm sharing the pH test log from our latest batch — and the one ingredient I almost kept that would have been a problem for reactive skin."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Slow to compound but produces the most durable trust moat.
- Requires consistent execution — missing a week is noticeable when you've established a documentary cadence.
- Highest ceiling of the three shapes; the most defensible long-term.
- Requires the founder to be comfortable showing imperfect work in public. If she is not, this shape does not work.

---

### Shape B — Customer-Witness-First

**Description:** The founder steps back as the primary voice and makes the first 50 real customers the center of the campaign. She emails all 420 subscribers asking for volunteers to share honest before-and-after accounts — not polished testimonials, raw descriptions of what changed and why. Publishes these with permission, with real names (first name at minimum) and real photos (optional). The founder's role is curator and amplifier, not protagonist.

**Flagship content (Self/Us/Now):**
- *Self:* "I started this because my own skin failed every product I tried. But I'm not the point. Here are the people whose skin I made it for."
- *Us:* "We've all read the reviews that could have been written by a copywriter. These weren't."
- *Now:* "I'm asking — if the serum changed something for you, will you let me share your words? Not a review. Just what happened."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Requires willing customers; depends on the quality and breadth of the existing list.
- Lower founder-exposure risk — customers do the heavy lifting.
- Strong UGC flywheel potential if photos are good; can seed to niche media.
- Slower start (must recruit witnesses before publishing); if the email to 420 subscribers produces fewer than 5 willing participants, the shape needs 2 more weeks of customer cultivation first.

---

### Shape C — Counter-Narrative-First

**Description:** The founder opens the campaign publicly with the unit economics story — why she stopped running Instagram ads, what the math looked like, and what she is doing instead. This positions the brand explicitly against the paid-influencer playbook that dominates the category. The content is aimed at the audience's known skepticism about sponsored content: "here is why you should not trust most of what you see in this category, and here is how to tell the difference."

**Flagship content (Self/Us/Now):**
- *Self:* "I spent €800 a month on Instagram ads. Every customer I acquired through them cost me €48 to reach. My margin is €22. I did this for four months before I stopped."
- *Us:* "If you've ever wondered why every skincare founder sounds the same — it's because they're all running the same paid playbook. You can see it in the language. 'Dermatologist-approved.' 'Science-backed.' 'Clean.' Same words, every brand."
- *Now:* "I stopped running ads three months ago. Here's what I'm doing instead and why I think it will work. I'll show you the numbers as I go."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Highest short-term earned-media potential — category transparency is genuinely newsworthy in beauty.
- Requires the founder to be comfortable sharing real financials in public. If she is not, this shape cannot be executed with authenticity.
- Creates a natural story arc: the campaign *is* the story, and outcomes become content.
- Risk: if the organic-first pivot does not produce visible results within 60 days, the public commitment creates accountability pressure.

**Recommendation:** Shape A as the primary structure, with Shape C's counter-narrative positioning as the opening move (Week 1 email to existing list) and Shape B's customer-witness mechanism running as the community engine from Month 2 onward.

---

| Write and send the counter-narrative email to the 420-person list: "Why I stopped running ads and what I'm doing instead." Include the real CPA/margin numbers. | Write Week 1 Formulation Log Instagram Reel: pick one ingredient, show the actual supplier spec, explain why it's in the formula and what it replaced in an earlier version. Shoot on phone, no studio. Post to Instagram Reels and cross-post to TikTok. | 2.5h | Founder | Saves ≥30 (organic signal gate); replies in comments asking follow-up ingredient questions |
| Research niche media targets: identify 3 publications or newsletters focused on clean beauty, sensitive skin, or DTC founder stories. Goal: produce a list with editor names, recent story angles, and contact method by Friday. (Target research is the action; no pitch this week.) | 1.5h | Founder | List of 3 specific targets with verified contact channel, produced before Week 2 |
| Set up UTM tagging for all campaign links: Instagram bio link, email CTAs, TikTok link-in-bio. Naming: utm_source=instagram / email / tiktok, utm_medium=organic, utm_campaign=formulation-log. | 1h | Founder | UTM parameters firing correctly in analytics; source breakdown visible in dashboard |
| Identify 10 customers from email list who have replied before or shown high engagement. Draft a personal, one-to-one email (not a broadcast) asking if they would share an honest account of their experience for the Witnesses series. | 2.5h | Founder | ≥3 positive replies within 7 days |

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Categorical asymmetry note:** Broad cold-paid Instagram or TikTok campaigns are refused. The math is clear from the brief (€48 CPA, €22 margin on cold audiences) and the asymmetry prohibits scale. The only paid surface permitted is warm retargeting of the existing email list custom audience on proven Reels.

**Creative direction for warm retargeting (email list only):**

- Pull directly from the Formulation Log Reels that have cleared the organic gate. Do not produce separate ad creative — use exactly the content that already demonstrated organic engagement. The ad is an amplifier of a proven signal, not a new signal.
- No studio re-production. The phone footage that worked organically works in the retargeting. Re-polishing it will make it look like an ad and lower performance with this audience.
- Copy tone: first-person, ingredient-specific. Not "try our serum." Something like: "I showed our pH test results on Instagram this week. If you're on our list and haven't tried the formulation log yet, here's where it is." Reference the specific piece being boosted.
- CTA: direct to the product page that matches the ingredient in the Reel, or to the newsletter archive. No generic homepage.

**24–48h organic traction gate (non-negotiable):**
A Reel must produce ≥30 saves (not likes) in the first 24–48 hours of organic posting before it is eligible for boosting. Saves signal that the content is worth keeping — they are a proxy for genuine interest, not reflexive scroll-past. If a Reel does not clear 30 saves in 48 hours, do not boost it. Produce a new one. The gate applies to every piece of content, every time.

**Audience definition:**
- Email list custom audience upload (Meta Ads Manager). Updated monthly.
- No lookalike expansion. No interest targeting. No cold audiences.
- Frequency cap: 4 impressions per person per 14-day window. Above 6 impressions, conversion rates fall and brand trust erodes. Refresh creative every 2 weeks.

**Budget cap:** €300/month hard limit on paid. If the warm retargeting is not producing measurable site traffic above organic baseline after 4 weeks, stop it and redirect to product seeding.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template 5 — Organic-source attribution (zero budget baseline) + micro-lift check on any paid retargeting.**

**Why Template 5:** The campaign is effectively zero paid for the first 30 days. Template 5 is the appropriate measurement tool for a trust-compounding, organic-first playbook at categorical asymmetry.

**Hypothesis:** Instagram Reels with formulation-transparency content produce a higher per-visitor conversion rate than any other organic channel currently in the stack (email vs. Instagram vs. TikTok if added in Month 2). Doubling effort on the highest-converting channel in Month 2 will produce a measurable lift in total purchases without any additional paid spend.

**Setup:**
- UTM-tag all links from Week 1 (see Week 1 action list above).
- Naming: `utm_source=instagram`, `utm_source=email`, `utm_source=tiktok`, `utm_medium=organic`, `utm_campaign=formulation-log`.
- Analytics: track source-level conversion rates (purchases ÷ unique visitors from that source) in the analytics tool already in use. If not currently tracking source-level conversion, set this up in Week 1 as a prerequisite.

**Duration:** 30 days minimum (Weeks 1–4). Extend to 60 days before making channel elimination decisions.

**Ranking and decision rule:**
- After 30 days, rank channels by conversion rate per unique visitor (not by total conversions or impressions).
- Top channel: double weekly effort in Month 2.
- Any channel performing at <20% of the top channel's conversion rate: drop unless it serves a demonstrable secondary function (e.g., a TikTok video that drives zero direct purchases but generates UGC from a creator who found the brand there — flag this and retain).
- If all channels produce <0.5% overall conversion rate: the bottleneck has moved upstream — run a fresh MMF check before investing further in distribution.

**For warm retargeting (if launched Week 3):**
Run Template 4 micro-lift: add "How did you hear about us?" to the post-purchase thank-you page. Options: Instagram / email newsletter / ad / friend recommendation / other. If ≤5% of converters credit the ad, the retargeting is providing no measurable lift — pause it.

**Caveat (state out loud):** Template 5 is directional, not causal. Multi-touch attribution is underspecified — a customer who bought via an email link may have first discovered the brand on Instagram. Treat the channel rankings as a rough guide to effort reallocation, not as proof of causality. If paid budget ever exceeds €1,000/month in Month 4+, graduate to Template 1 (geo-holdout) for real incremental measurement.

**Decision threshold for Month 3:**
- Purchases: are total monthly purchases growing month-over-month without proportional increase in paid spend? If yes: the organic flywheel is compounding. Accelerate Tier 1–2 effort.
- Email list growth: is the list growing from organic referrals (not just from the existing subscriber base)? Target: net +50 new subscribers/month by Month 2, net +100/month by Month 4.
- Repeat purchase rate (90-day): track from Month 3 onward. A brand with a trust moat has higher repeat rates than a brand with paid acquisition — this is the metric that matters most for the €15k/month revenue goal by Month 9.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

**Track these (with reporting cadence):**

| Metric | Cadence | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Instagram Reel saves (not likes) | Per post, 24–48h | The organic traction gate. Saves signal content worth keeping. Likes are reflexive. |
| Email open rate and reply rate | Per send | 28% open rate is your baseline; protect it. Reply rate is the trust signal — it means the email started a conversation, not a scroll. |
| Per-channel conversion rate (purchases ÷ unique visitors, UTM-tagged) | Monthly | The Template 5 ranking that drives effort reallocation. |
| 90-day repeat purchase rate | Monthly from Month 3 | The trust-moat metric. Rising repeat rate at flat acquisition cost means the flywheel is working. |
| Organic UGC volume and quality | Monthly | Count posts from real customers (not paid). Check if they include specific product language (a sign of genuine engagement rather than generic "love this!"). |
| Email list net new subscribers per month (organic referrals only) | Monthly | Referral-driven list growth means the content is spreading. If list only grows from paid or gated opt-ins, it is not compounding. |
| Direct replies or DMs from Instagram and email | Weekly | Qualitative trust signal. People who DM the founder are bottom-of-funnel customers; they are one conversation away from a purchase. |
| Contribution margin per channel (post-COGS, post-shipping, post-refunds) | Monthly | The unit economics check. If a channel is driving purchases at negative margin, it does not matter how the platform reports it. |

**Ignore these explicitly:**

| Metric | Why to ignore |
|---|---|
| Instagram follower count | Followers do not purchase. Engagement rate and saves are predictors; follower count is a vanity number the algorithm does not even weight highly. |
| Reach and impressions | Reach above 5–6 impressions does not persuade; it annoys. Impressions are the competitor's game; do not play it. |
| Platform-reported ROAS or attributed conversions | Meta attributes a conversion to any ad the user saw in the last N days. Attributed ROAS overstates incremental ROAS by 2–10× in most campaigns (Lewis & Rao 2015, QJE). The Template 4 micro-lift check is the honest signal. |
| Total likes across any platform | Cheap, often bot-inflated, and uncorrelated with purchase intent. Saves, replies, and shares are the proxies for intent. |
| TikTok follower count (if added) | Same principle: it is a lagging and noisy indicator. Track Reel saves and conversion rate instead. |
| Total monthly email subscribers (without open rate segmentation) | A list of 5,000 unengaged subscribers is worth less than a list of 420 at 28% open rate. Do not trade open rate quality for list quantity. |

---

*Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table and I will re-run the affected stages.*
